high_concern_flags: factual_uncertainty:prompt_correction (informational): Sprint prompt asserted California is a 300-day-clock state implicitly via reference to federal-law layering. Verified explicitly — California IS a 300-day EEOC deferral state (CRD has work-sharing agreement with EEOC), which is the OPPOSITE of Alabama/Arkansas (both 180-day non-deferral). Page reflects 300-day clock correctly.

factual_uncertainty:prompt_addition (informational): Sprint prompt did not mention SB 617 Cal-WARN content amendments effective 1 January 2026. Verified against leginfo (chaptered SB 617), Littler ASAP, KingsleyKingsley, CDF Labor Law, EDD WSIN25-14, Mondaq, Lexology, and National Law Review — bill chaptered late 2025, amends Cal. Lab. Code § 1401, adds three mandatory notice content elements (workforce-board coordination statement, WDB contact, CalFresh program info). Page leads with SB 617 as the 2026 highlight on Cal-WARN; deficient-notice exposure is the standout PAGA-adjacent risk. Compliance cache for California (master-0028) covered payroll-side only; this child page is the first to surface SB 617.

factual_uncertainty:prompt_addition (informational): Sprint prompt described the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing without distinguishing the 1988 Foley framing from the 2000 Guz narrowing. Page adds Guz v. Bechtel Nat'l, Inc., 24 Cal.4th 317 (2000) as the dispositive narrowing case — the implied covenant in employment is contract-bounded and does NOT support tort damages (no California analogue to insurance bad-faith). This is the modern California employer-defence benchmark.
